summary: Today's sweep leans toward AI / computing, Democracy / press freedom, CS education / AI policy. The strongest items in this run are Google is testing AI chatbot search for YouTube, Valve’s new Steam Controller isn’t perfect, but I’m buying one anyway, and OpenAI ends its exclusive partnership with Microsoft.

mainItems.title: Google is testing AI chatbot search for YouTube
mainItems.summary: Google is trying out an AI Mode-like search experience for YouTube.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Valve’s new Steam Controller isn’t perfect, but I’m buying one anyway
mainItems.summary: After my brief hands-on last year with Valve's new Steam Controller, I said it might be my dream controller.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: OpenAI ends its exclusive partnership with Microsoft
mainItems.summary: Amended agreement clears the way for OpenAI models to run on Amazon Bedrock.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Google employees ask Sundar Pichai to say no to classified military AI use
mainItems.summary: Over 600 Google employees signed a letter to CEO Sundar Pichai demanding that Google block the Pentagon from using its AI models for classified purposes, reports the The Washington Post.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Man charged with killing Florida doctoral students allegedly consulted ChatGPT
mainItems.summary: Hisham Abugharbieh has been charged in the deaths of his roommate and his roommate’s girlfriend Sign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inbox The man charged with killing two University of South Florida…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: Baby on board: US woman gives birth on Delta flight
mainItems.summary: Paramedics on Atlanta-Portland flight help Ashley Blair give birth to ‘gorgeous’ Brielle Renee just before landing When baby Brielle Renee was born, new mom Ashley Blair was flying high – quite literally.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: US supreme court weighs blocking lawsuits against Roundup makers alleging weedkiller causes cancer
mainItems.summary: Case centers on glyphosate, pesticide used in Roundup and other products that has been linked to cancer in some studies Members of the US supreme court peppered lawyers for the former Monsanto Company with a barrage of questions over…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.title: Mass Effect: Andromeda actor says the game "was done dirty" by EA and "a very toxic atmosphere" online
mainItems.summary: It's been over nine years since Mass Effect: Andromeda tried to kickstart a new era of BioWare's original sci-fi RPG universe, an experiment that ended with pretty poor results.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: Opinion: The Trump Administration Says Literacy Matters. Its Budget Plan Says Otherwise
mainItems.summary: Two months after Donald Trump swore in Linda McMahon as secretary of education, she named “evidence-based literacy” as one of the administration’s top three priorities.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: Google DeepMind Paper Argues LLMs Will Never Be Conscious
mainItems.summary: Philosophers said the paper’s argument is sound, but that “all these arguments have been presented years and years ago.”
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: Musk vs. Altman: Tech CEOs head to court over the fate of OpenAI
mainItems.summary: The former OpenAI business partners are embroiled in a high-stakes dispute over the future of one of the world's top AI companies.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Interesting where engineering, science, and tech infrastructure collide.

mainItems.title: Mother Mary Might Just Be the Experience You’re Waiting For
mainItems.summary: Is… is this what it feels like to be the invisible default? The post Mother Mary Might Just Be the Experience You’re Waiting For appeared first on Reactor .
mainItems.whyItMatters: On-theme for your cultural interests, especially when the concept is strong.

mainItems.title: South Carolina's measles outbreak is over. But more are brewing around the country
mainItems.summary: The virus infected nearly 1,000 people in the state before the state declared it over. Meanwhile, cases are spreading across many parts of the U.S., with more than 20 outbreaks currently active.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: 12 unexpected sourdough bread recipes that aren't crusty loaves: From sourdough flatbread to sourdough milk bread — and even sourdough babka.
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing, the kind of bread-nerd rabbit hole worth keeping.

highlights.title: A.I. Is Prompting an Evolution, Not Extinction, for Coders
highlights.notes: Interesting piece on the current state of AI in software work.

highlights.title: The Workers Letting A.I. Do Their Jobs
highlights.notes: In an era of agents powered by artificial intelligence, many programmers are barely programming.

highlights.title: Trump’s presidency is what evil looks like: absurd, frightening, cruel
highlights.notes: Particularly strong Guardian opinion piece, noted for future briefing highlights.
